projects.title: Surface Surveys
projects.narrative: Surface Surveys is a six-part photographic body of work made between 2015 and 2019 in remote and geologically significant terrains across four continents. The project studies how the Earth's surfaces record elemental force: sand, ice, lava, gypsum, granite, and parallel dune systems.
projects.location: Oman; Idaho; Greenland; New Mexico; Australia
projects.process: Aerial and ground-based photographic survey with medium-format Leica S007
projects.visualDescription: ice, sand, lava, gypsum, granite, elemental, ambiguous scale

descriptionSections.scientific: 2015-2019; six landscapes; four continents; medium-format Leica S007; aerial and ground-based surveys; three first artist-led aerial survey distinctions
descriptionSections.visual: elemental, abstract, evidentiary, mineral, glacial, volcanic, granular, wind-shaped, line-driven
descriptionSections.geographic: Greenland Ice Sheet; White Sands; Craters of the Moon; Simpson Desert; Wahiba Sands; City of Rocks
descriptionSections.atmospheric: remote, exposed, silent, glaring, wind-marked, cold, hot, immense
descriptionSections.spatial: aerial fields, close surfaces, vast terrain, compressed distance, serial landscape chapters

artworkAtoms.material: Archival pigment print on Canson Baryta Photographique II
artworkAtoms.dimensions: Variable by selected Surface Surveys artwork

projects.title: Surface Surveys: Greenland
projects.narrative: Greenland is the ice-sheet chapter of Surface Surveys. Within a project concerned with planetary surfaces shaped by elemental force, Greenland turns frozen water into surface, archive, terrain, and abstraction. It connects the survey's larger desert logic to glacial scale: meltwater, crevasse, sediment, blue pools, white fields, and the visible record of climate held in ice.
projects.process: Aerial photographic survey and field observation of ice, meltwater, crevasses, sediment, and glacial surface systems
projects.visualDescription: glacial, ice, meltwater pools, turquoise, cobalt, fine dark lines

descriptionSections.scientific: 2015 and 2017; Greenland Ice Sheet; largest ice sheet outside Antarctica; Ilulissat Icefjord context; first woman to complete a photographic aerial survey of the Greenland Ice Sheet in 2017
descriptionSections.visual: white, cobalt, turquoise, granular, fractured, pooled, veined, crystalline, sediment-speckled
descriptionSections.geographic: Greenland Ice Sheet; Ilulissat Icefjord; Arctic glacial terrain
descriptionSections.atmospheric: cold, remote, luminous, severe, quiet, suspended, immense
descriptionSections.spatial: aerial ice fields, crevasse networks, meltwater basins, broad white planes, blue channel systems

artworkAtoms.material: Archival pigment print on Canson Baryta Photographique II

projects.title: Surface Surveys: White Sands
projects.narrative: White Sands is the gypsum chapter of Surface Surveys. It brings the project into restricted airspace over the world's largest gypsum dunefield, where mineral brightness, military boundary, basin geology, and aerial access make white surface both landscape and threshold.
projects.location: White Sands National Park, New Mexico, USA
projects.process: Aerial photographic survey of gypsum dunes, restricted airspace, white mineral sand, basin systems, and glare
projects.visualDescription: gypsum, plaster, blinding white, dripping plaster, meringue, soft folds

descriptionSections.scientific: 2018; world's largest gypsum dunefield; about 275 square miles; Tularosa Basin; restricted airspace; first artist-led photographic aerial survey of the gypsum dunefield
descriptionSections.visual: white, cool-toned, luminous, powdery, rippled, glaring, pale, wind-drawn
descriptionSections.geographic: White Sands National Park; Tularosa Basin; New Mexico; White Sands Missile Range and Holloman Air Force Base context
descriptionSections.atmospheric: bright, exposed, silent, reflective, austere, high-glare
descriptionSections.spatial: dune fields, basin floor, aerial distance, repeated ridges, open white expanse

artworkAtoms.material: Archival pigment print on Canson Baryta Photographique II

projects.title: Surface Surveys: Wahiba
projects.narrative: Wahiba is the Oman sand-sea chapter of Surface Surveys. It places the larger project inside a living desert system where wind, monsoon cycles, Bedouin routes, sand color, and open night sky make surface both geological record and inhabited terrain.
projects.process: Ground-based desert field survey of wind-shaped sand, settlement routes, color, and surface movement

projects.title: Surface Surveys: Craters
projects.narrative: Craters is the volcanic Idaho chapter of Surface Surveys and the local anchor for The Observatory commission. It translates the basalt surface of Craters of the Moon into evidence, abstraction, darkness, and mineral memory, connecting the global survey back to Central Idaho's lava field and dark-sky terrain.

projects.process: Aerial and ground-based survey of basaltic lava field, cinder cones, spatter cones, fissures, sagebrush plains, and dark-sky terrain

projects.title: Surface Surveys: Simpson Desert
projects.narrative: Simpson Desert is the parallel-dune chapter of Surface Surveys. It expands the project into the scale of Australia's Munga-Thirri sand country, where long dune ridges, traditional Country, aerial distance, and red surface repetition become the structure of the work.
projects.process: Aerial photographic survey of long parallel dunes, red sand systems, desert scale, and dune-line repetition

projects.title: Surface Surveys: City of Rocks
projects.narrative: City of Rocks is the granite chapter of Surface Surveys. It brings the body of work into a sculptural Idaho landscape where spires, fins, domes, monoliths, historic trail language, and exposed geologic time turn stone into vertical surface.

projects.process: Ground-based granite field survey of spires, fins, domes, monoliths, sagebrush, and weathered stone surfaces

projects.title: Omani Landscapes
projects.narrative: Omani Landscapes began at the dawn of generative AI, asking a single question: what is a photograph when an image can be spun up from a prompt? Each work starts as a photograph Lisa made in Oman with a Leica, then strips that image down through software until only its underlying patterns remain — and rebuilds those patterns into something new that no longer looks anything like a photograph. The result is a body of work that is genuinely photographic in origin yet reads as pure abstraction. Where generative AI can now produce images that look like photographs but are not, these are photographs that do not look like photographs — posing the question at the center of the project: is a photograph defined by form (what it looks like) or by function (how it was made)?
projects.location: Oman; Jebel Akhdar and desert landscapes
projects.medium: Photography (Leica capture, software pattern-extraction and reconstruction)
projects.process: Photographs made in Oman with a Leica are reduced in software to their underlying patterns, then rebuilt into new, non-photographic abstractions.
projects.visualDescription: pink, green, feminine, bread browns, earth tones, dissolved landform, form versus function

descriptionSections.scientific: 2024; Oman (Leica source photographs); software pattern-extraction then reconstruction; made at the dawn of generative AI; interrogates form vs function in photography
descriptionSections.visual: saturated, pointillist, granular, graphic, abstract, pink, green, violet, dissolved
descriptionSections.geographic: Oman; Jebel Akhdar; desert and mountain terrain
descriptionSections.spatial: dissolved landforms, dune and mountain silhouettes, panoramic abstraction, color-field pattern

artworkAtoms.material: Archival pigment print on Hahnemühle bright white baryta
artworkAtoms.dimensions: Variable / large-format by selected Omani Landscapes artwork (working files at 200 ppi)

projects.title: Winterblue
projects.narrative:
Winterblue was born from a winter search for Lisa's missing family dog. For 11 days she and her community searched. Sleeping in her car, searching in whiteouts, she met the full force of winter in the form of fear, isolation, temperature, and overwhelming discomfort. The dog was found unharmed.

Winterblue I is presented as a large-scale contact sheet — a grid of 125 individual photographs, one set of frames for each day spent on the Camas Prairie. The project carries regional character of embracing discomfort into winter atmosphere and emotional terrain.

The work demonstrates the practice — entering difficulty deliberately, staying long enough for perception to shift, and returning with images shaped by that exposure.
projects.process: 17 trips between January and April to the Camas Prairie under sub-zero conditions turned darkness, cold, isolation, and grief into a field of attention.
projects.visualDescription: midnight blue, indigo, ice blue, monochromatic, snow, wind-carved drifts, moonlight, sub-zero

descriptionSections.scientific: 2023; Camas Prairie, Idaho; 17 trips January-April under sub-zero conditions; large-scale contact sheet of 125 photographs
descriptionSections.visual: blue, white, wintered, snow-bound, gridded, serial, sparse, cold-toned
descriptionSections.geographic: Camas Prairie; Idaho; high prairie; Wood River Valley character
descriptionSections.atmospheric: cold, isolated, severe, quiet, grief-marked, whiteout, sub-zero, enduring
descriptionSections.spatial: contact-sheet grid, 125 daily frames, large-scale wall field, 138 x 85 inch panel

artworkAtoms.material: Archival pigment print on Hahnemühle bright white baryta; mounted on painted maple wood

projects.title: Flipped
projects.narrative:
A minimalist photographic work that renders a flipped iceberg as the visible inversion of class power: blue rises, white sinks.

AI, robotics, and accelerated automation are redrawing social order. Blue-collar work gains status and leverage while routine white-collar roles compress. The iceberg becomes a clean grammar for that shift. Traditional ice shows white above and deep blue below. A flipped iceberg reverses those planes. The surface turns blue. White drops under the line.

FLIPPED reads the present and projects the near future: AI and automation shift power from routine white-collar administration to blue work—build, repair, operate. LISA WOOD STUDIO grounds the argument in two sources: medium-format photographs from two Greenland expeditions and AI images authored through precise prompts. The pair creates a single visual grammar: a flipped iceberg where deep blue rises above the line and white drops below it. Ice and light carry the claim without rhetoric. The sequence lands the inversion in one clean beat and holds on the new order.
projects.medium: Large format photographs (Leica Medium Format + AI Generated)
projects.process: I author prompts, lock seeds, and curate outputs as I would exposures. I treat AI as a controlled imaging instrument, not a theme. Medium-format RAW files from Greenland; high-resolution AI images generated from the artist’s prompts; unified tone curve, horizon, and color discipline to form one body.
projects.visualDescription: cinematic, monumental, icebergs, luminous blue-white, looming peaks, black mirror water, backlit, moody, otherworldly, inverted

artworkAtoms.material: Archival pigment print on Hahnemühle bright white baryta

projects.title: Palouse
projects.narrative: Palouse is a photographic study of the farmed landscape of the Palouse region of the inland Pacific Northwest, where wind-deposited loess has built a terrain of deep, rolling hills given almost entirely to dryland agriculture. The work reads the cultivated surface as a record of both elemental force and human labor — contour-plowed slopes, seasonal color, and the long geometry of wheat, barley, and legume fields shaped to the undulation of the land.
projects.location: Palouse region, southeastern Washington and northern Idaho, USA
projects.process: Photographic survey of the cultivated Palouse landscape, made with a Nikon D800.
projects.visualDescription: loess hills, wind-blown silt, rolling, corduroy furrows, green, brown, spare

descriptionSections.scientific: shot circa 2013 (artist recollection); Nikon D800; Palouse region; rolling loess hills up to ~500 ft over Columbia River Basalt; wind-deposited Pleistocene glacial silt; one of the most productive non-irrigated (dryland) farming regions in the world; largest U.S. lentil-growing region
descriptionSections.visual: rolling, contoured, striped, cultivated, seasonal, green-to-gold, sculpted, undulating, patterned
descriptionSections.geographic: Palouse region; southeastern Washington; northern Idaho; south of Spokane to the Snake River; Whitman County; Latah County
descriptionSections.atmospheric: open, pastoral, expansive, weathered, quiet, seasonal, sunlit
descriptionSections.spatial: rolling loess hills, contour-plowed slopes, field geometry, layered ridgelines, broad agricultural distance

artworkAtoms.material: Archival pigment print on Hahnemühle bright white baryta

projects.title: Side Effects
projects.narrative:
SIDE EFFECTS introduces a prescribing cascade in which one drug is introduced to treat the side effect of another drug, connecting 4 prescription drugs.
The drugs profiled within the cascade are imaginary; their listed side effects are not. Each side effect is approved for one or more prescription drugs in the US by the United States Food and Drug Administration.
SIDE EFFECTS looks at the FDA and Big Pharma’s role in perpetuating prescribing cascades in a society molded by increased expectations and diminished timelines.
Rather than invest in making current drugs safer let us regard side effects as symptoms and sell and consume new drugs to treat them.
SIDE EFFECTS is not a negation of the pharmaceutical industry; it's a push for increased responsibility.

artworkAtoms.dimensions: Two Wall Murals - 49ft W X 15ft H

projects.title: Attention
projects.narrative:
We have limitless insight into the daily interests and activities of friends, acquaintances, and strangers alike thanks to the vast reach of social media. Our thoughts, interests, and attention form the coveted treasure at the end of the advertisers' rainbow.
Yet, when viewed in  the context of global events shaping the 21st century, how well do we understand the thoughts of others? ATTENTION chronicles the most popular search queries annually, starting from 2001, the first year of available data, through 2021.
In hindsight of significant national and global events, ATTENTION represents 20 years of allegorical insight into what's been on our minds.
The five-minute recording illuminates each of the twenty year's most popular keywords globally as well as the most common "how-to" and "what-is" queries within the United States.
The compilation reflects Google's advancement in data analytics over 2 decades: in 2001, Google released top keyword search data worldwide. Beginning in 2006, the company created Google Trends, introducing numerous sub-search categories such as top people search, news & entertainment stories, top searches by region & country, and others.

artworkAtoms.dimensions: Gramophone: 6.25 sq ft X 6.85 ft H; Wall Text Decal: 30ft W X 22ft H; Mp3 Audio: 5:09 min

projects.title: ECS
projects.narrative:
In the clash between science and politics, an impossibly absurd anecdote reveals itself.
Serving as metaphor, ECS contemplates the history of the United States Controlled Substances Act as it relates to private vs. public interests, discrimination, and fear.
The endogenous cannabinoid system, named for the plant that led to its discovery, is one of the most important physiologic systems responsible for establishing and maintaining human health.

artworkAtoms.dimensions: LED Screen: 13ft W X 4ft H X 3.5in D

projects.title: Mind the Gap
projects.narrative:
"Between the conception
And the creation
Between the emotion
And the response
Falls the Shadow" - excerpt from The Hollow Men by T.S Eliot ,1925

MIND | FIND THE GAP directs our attention toward the space that carries the energy of change - to the transient, inscrutable place between that which we know.
to the realm that exists 'between the idea and the reality'
to the place between life as we know it and life as we never imagined
to the space between thought and action
to the place that elevates or swallows
where fortune & fortunes are lost and found
to the place that 'Falls the Shadow'

artworkAtoms.dimensions: NEON: 15ft W X 26ft W X 4ft H

projects.title: Luxuriate in Discomfort
projects.narrative:
Luxuriate in discomfort is a central philosophy for Lisa Wood and a theme running through her practice — stepping into challenging conditions and staying there long enough for perception to change. It is a studio philosophy carried across three projects: a book, a one-night installation, and a public art project concept to improve teenage mental health.

Luxuriate in Discomfort considers our relationship with discomfort, and how the relationship shapes our experience. Together, the works confront our pursuit of comfort and luxury via a double entendre: by living a life of luxury in day-to-day torment, and by seeking discomfort as a means toward contentment. Born out of a 2.5 year inquiry into the nature of pain, the work asks what is luxury's ultimate prize, is it freedom?
projects.medium: Studio philosophy across a book, a one-night installation, and a public art concept

descriptionSections.scientific: 2020–2024; eight-year personal discomfort practice; three projects — book (2020), one-night installation (2020), LUX public art concept (2024); modalities: sauna, cold plunge, whole-body vibration
descriptionSections.visual: blue, lullaby-toned, mirrored, double-entendre, satirical, intimate
descriptionSections.geographic: Sun Valley, Idaho; Wood River Valley
descriptionSections.spatial: bedroom installation, vending machines, neon, sculpture, book, poetry, painting, video

projects.title: Luxuriate in Discomfort: Book
projects.narrative:
Luxuriate In Discomfort is a 30-page digest that explores how regular engagement with discomfort can lead to long-term contentment, written by the artist in 2020 for her eldest son as he begins to navigate the world post college, graduating into a pandemic-induced lockdown. It is a compilation of thoughts and ideas that emerged during the artist's water sessions over the past 2.5 years.

The book is written one reflective line per page, building from "cultivating a thirst for discomfort cultivates the way to personal fulfillment" toward the closing line, "ask a lot of yourself."

descriptionSections.scientific: 2020; 30-page digest; 7 inches square; one reflective line per page; written for the artist's eldest son

projects.title: Luxuriate in Discomfort: December, Sun Valley
projects.narrative:
Luxuriating In Discomfort is a group of works born out of a 2.5 year inquiry into the nature of sadness and pain. Comprised of poetry, a book, a large painting, and a video clip, the work is autobiographical in nature, and serves as the culmination of a period of grief and transformation that began on July 1, 2018, the day I resolved to approach my own self-care with sincerity.

From use of the bedroom as installation space to employing a lullaby palette, Luxuriating In Discomfort dwells in double entendres, touching on both the multi-dimensionality and the potentiality of our experience. Water emerges as a primary theme, as both subject matter in the poems and as visual metaphor in the painting, memorializing the elemental medium that opened the pathway towards transformation.

A one-night-only exhibition staged inside the artist's bedroom in Sun Valley, Idaho in December 2020 at the height of the pandemic, opened to a small masked, distanced audience. Works on view: a mixed media painting, poetry (HEAT and COLD), the book Luxuriate in Discomfort, and a video clip (Hedge Crawl, from Andy Goldsworthy's Leaning into the Wind).
projects.medium: One-night multimedia installation (mixed media painting, poetry, book, video)

descriptionSections.scientific: December 2020; Sun Valley, Idaho; one-night-only bedroom installation; works: mixed media painting (begun as a year painting January 1, 2019), HEAT & COLD poems, book, video
descriptionSections.visual: blue, lullaby-palette, watery, layered, textured, intimate
descriptionSections.geographic: Sun Valley, Idaho; Wood River Valley
descriptionSections.spatial: bedroom as gallery, painting above the bed, poems, book, projected video

artworkAtoms.material: Mixed media painting; poetry; artist book; video
artworkAtoms.dimensions: HEAT poem: 101in W X 131in H; COLD poem: 91.5in W X 87.5in H

projects.title: Luxuriate in Discomfort: LUX
projects.narrative:
LUX is a public art installation concept that addresses the teenage mental health crisis through an intentional discomfort practice: cold plunge, sauna, and whole-body vibration. Built on the artist's own eight-year practice, LUX offers young adults a constructive, approachable framework for building a working relationship with adversity — pairing physical rejuvenation with the psychological reward that follows.

LUX is a metaphorical satire of the LAX sign found at the entrance to Los Angeles International Airport, and references Southern California's historical and current place as a global trendsetter in what it means to live an extraordinarily comfortable life. HEAT and COLD symbolize oppression and rejection personified by excessively hot and cold environments and present an alternate path toward achieving comfort and freedom. Two vending machines dispense the book Luxuriate in Discomfort.

The project is structured around three movements — Paradox, Practice, and Gesture — and grew from the artist's personal connection to the young adult mental health crisis.

descriptionSections.scientific: 2024; public art concept; teenage mental health; three modalities — sauna, cold plunge, whole-body vibration; structured as Paradox / Practice / Gesture
descriptionSections.geographic: concept references LAX / Southern California; Sun Valley origin
descriptionSections.spatial: stainless sculpture, vending machines, neon, book distribution, AR portal

artworkAtoms.dimensions: LUX Sculpture: 26.5ft L X 21ft W X 13ft H; Two Vending Machines: 4.5ft W X 6.5ft H X 3.5ft D; Luxuriate in Discomfort Neon: 111in W X 6.5in H
